text: The surgical setup includes a robotic system docked from the patient's right shoulder, with three robotic arms and a 12mm assistant port. The red circle marks the target anatomy location, and the setup accommodates an ultrasound probe.

The surgeon performs a wide Cocker maneuver, suspending the duodenum from four corners. A laparoscopic ultrasound is used to localize the ampulla. A longitudinal duodenotomy is created, and two stitches are placed superiorly and inferiorly to the ampulla.

The surgeon uses stitches to mobilize and retract the duodenum while performing circumferential dissection down to the level of the pancreas. Monopolar scissors are utilized for precise tissue cutting during this dissection.
The surgeon identifies the biliary and pancreatic ducts and transects them using scissors without applying energy. A diagram illustrates the simplified reconstruction process.
The surgeon uses POTS scissors to spatulate the transected common bile duct, followed by performing a choledochoduodenostomy with interrupted V-O vial stitches.
The surgeon secures the common bile duct to the pancreatic duct, forming a new septum between these structures.

The surgeon uses IVO bicryl to take full-thickness bites of the pancreas and duodenum in a running fashion, repairing the duodenal defect. The running stitch is applied on both sides, visibly incorporating the duodenal mucosa to repair the defect.
The surgeon begins a second running stitch on the contralateral side, running it inferiorly to meet and tie with the previously placed stitch, completing the closure.
The surgeon ties together the two sutures that have been run inferiorly, completing the reconstruction of the posterior wall of the duodenum.
The surgeon closes the duodenal anatomy using a two-layer closure technique. The first layer is secured with a 4-0 running bicryl suture, ensuring a tight and precise closure of the tissue.
The surgeon closes the second layer of the dual anatomy using a 3-0 bicurl Connell stitch, completing the closure with a view of the final appearance of the repaired anatomical structures.
